title: FOUNDATIONS - Lesson 4: The Doctrine of the Word
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: FOUNDATIONS - Lesson 4 - The Doctrine of the Word - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: What Is Revelation?
subpoints: Revelation means the unveiling of something not previously known, initiated by God, not discovered by man
In theological terms, revelation is God disclosing His character, purpose, and will to His creation
Revelation has a redemptive or damning purpose: the gospel is either the aroma of life or of death
Neo-Orthodoxy (Barth, Bultmann, Brunner) wrongly separated revelation from historical accuracy; the Bible presents both as inseparable

outline.title: God Reveals Himself Through Covenants
subpoints: God has not given abstract lectures about Himself but has always addressed us in the context of covenant obligations and promises
Key covenants: Covenant of Works (Adam), Covenant of Redemption (Genesis 3:15), Covenant with Noah, Covenant with Abraham, Mosaic Covenant, Davidic Covenant, New Covenant
Each successive covenant reveals more of God's character, purposes, and redemptive plan
The seed of Genesis 3:15 progresses through Shem, Abraham, Judah, Jesse, David, and ultimately to Jesus Christ

outline.title: Modes of Divine Revelation
subpoints: God revealed Himself through direct speech, theophanies (the Angel of the Lord as pre-incarnate Christ), dreams and visions
Other modes: angels, prophets, the Urim and Thummim, and acts of providence (manna, plagues, the Exodus)
The Angel of the Lord in the Old Testament uniquely accepted worship, identifying Him as the pre-incarnate Second Person of the Trinity
The ultimate revelation is the incarnation: Jesus Christ is the final and complete revelation of God (Hebrews 1:1-3; Revelation 1)

outline.title: The Old Testament as God's Word
subpoints: The expression 'Thus says the Lord' or 'the word of the Lord came' appears over 2,000 times in the Old Testament
Commands to preserve the books (in the Ark of the Covenant and beside it) show the high regard God and His people had for the written Word
The prophets spoke with divine authority; disobedience to them brought curse and judgment
The circular argument: we use the Bible to prove the Bible - this is a matter of faith, not circular fallacy, ultimately confirmed by the Holy Spirit

outline.title: Introduction to Scripture as Inspired Revelation
subpoints: All of what we have been studying about the modes and covenants of revelation resulted in the written Bible
The Bible is the Word of God not because it 'speaks to us' but because it is what it is, regardless of our response
It is a circular argument, but one resting on the Holy Spirit's conviction, not on logical self-proof
Next sessions will examine what the New Testament says about the Old Testament and the doctrine of inspiration

summary:
This session opens the Foundations series' treatment of the Doctrine of the Word (Bibliology), the fourth foundational doctrine after Creation, Providence, and Anthropology. Pastor Tito Lyro begins by defining revelation as God's self-disclosure to His creation - an act He initiates, not something humanity discovers on its own. He contrasts this with the Neo-Orthodox theology of Barth, Bultmann, and Brunner, which separated the revelatory function of Scripture from its historical accuracy, arguing instead that both are inseparably linked.

The bulk of the session explores how God has progressively revealed Himself through a series of covenants: the Covenant of Works with Adam, the Covenant of Redemption announced in Genesis 3:15, the Noahic covenant of preservation, the Abrahamic covenant of promise, the Mosaic covenant of law, the Davidic covenant of the kingdom, and finally the New Covenant which fulfills and surpasses them all. Each covenant adds to our understanding of who God is and how He deals with humanity in redemption.

Lyro then surveys the various modes through which God communicated His revelation: direct speech, theophanies (especially the Angel of the Lord as pre-incarnate Christ), dreams, visions, angels, prophets, the Urim and Thummim, and acts of providence. He argues that whenever God appeared in bodily form in the Old Testament, it was the Second Person of the Trinity - the one who would later become incarnate as Jesus Christ. The ultimate and final revelation is the incarnation itself, as affirmed in Hebrews 1:1-3.

The session closes with an introduction to the Old Testament's self-witness as the Word of God - the repeated authoritative formulas, the commanded preservation of the books, and the authority of the prophets - and acknowledges that the argument for Scripture's authority from Scripture is circular but rests ultimately on the convicting work of the Holy Spirit.
transcript:
We're going to continue our Foundations series where we're looking at 12 different doctrines that are foundational to the Christian faith. So far we've looked at the doctrine of Creation, the doctrine of Providence, and the doctrine of Anthropology. Today we're going to start looking at the doctrine of the Word. Some call it Bibliology. The doctrine of the Word covers how we got the Bible, what the Bible is, what should be in the Bible, and so on. We're going to begin by looking at the idea of revelation — not the book of Revelation, but the concept of revelation itself.

## What Is Revelation?

The word *revelation* carries with it the idea of the unveiling of something not known before. In the theological sense, revelation denotes God's revealing His character, purpose, and will to His creation. The manifestation of God is something that He initiates, not something that mankind discovers on its own. Our confession says that if God had not initiated and revealed Himself to us, we would never have figured out on our own who He was. Not only did God create us in His image, but also out of His infinite mercy and compassion, He chose to disclose Himself to us, which He did not have to do.

David writes: *When I look at your heavens, the work of your fingers, the moon, and the stars which you have set in place, what is man that you are mindful of him? And the son of man that you care for him?* Perhaps as a shepherd boy laying on a grassy hill in the Judean wilderness, David looked up at the stars and recognized that God had made all of that — and yet, despite our smallness, God chose to reveal Himself to us.

Revelation has a redemptive or damning purpose. The heavens declare the glory of God, and if we don’t respond to that revelation which everyone can see, condemnation is the result. As Paul says in the Corinthian letters, the gospel always has an aroma — to some it is the aroma of life, and to some it is the aroma of death.

Now, one of the most influential theological movements of the 20th century — Neo-Orthodoxy, associated with Karl Barth, Rudolf Bultmann, and Emil Brunner — said that what matters is that the Bible reveals God, not that the Bible is historically accurate. It didn’t matter, they said, whether Jonah was actually swallowed by a great fish, or whether Abraham really left Ur of the Chaldees. What mattered was that those accounts disclose God. But that is not what the Bible says about itself. Because it is accurate — it is a disclosure of God in accurate, historically real terms. Those things actually happened.

## God Reveals Himself Through Covenants

In God’s revelation, He chose to reveal Himself by way of covenant. As one theologian has said, a remarkable thing about the way God has revealed Himself to us is that He has never given us abstract lectures about His person to satisfy our intellectual curiosity. He has always addressed us in the context of our responsibility to Him and His promises and warnings to us. His revelation has come to us in the form of covenant.

Our confession says at chapter 7: *The distance between God and the creature is so great that although reasonable creatures owe obedience to Him as their Creator, yet they could never have any enjoyment of Him as their blessedness and reward, but by some voluntary condescension on God’s part, which He has been pleased to express by way of covenant.*

So we find this revelation of God through the covenants: the Covenant of Works with Adam, the Covenant of Redemption, the Covenant with Noah, the Covenant with Abraham, the Mosaic Covenant, the Davidic Covenant, and the New Covenant. All of these are revelations of who God is, His being, His character, and what He expects of us.

### The Covenant of Works

God made a covenant with Adam, called the Covenant of Works or the Covenant of Creation. The second person of the Trinity — later known as the Lord Jesus Christ — revealed Himself to Adam, walking in the garden of Eden, saying: these are the things you must do, this is how I will bless you, and this is what will happen if you do not obey Me. By the way, every time God speaks to someone in a way that engages their senses — visually, audibly — it is the second person of the Trinity who speaks. He is the agent of revelation. As Colossians 1 says, He is the expressed image of the Godhead, the one who reveals the Godhead to us.

### The Covenant of Redemption

Right after the Fall, the Lord came looking for Adam and Eve — not to condemn, but ready to proclaim redemption. Notice whenever tragedy occurred in the Old Testament, God would speak. He immediately intervened to begin the process of redemption.

In Genesis 3:15 we have the seed of the woman who is going to crush Satan’s head. From that seed the line comes: through Shem (after Noah), then to Abraham — a pagan called out of Ur of the Chaldees — who was promised that through him God would bless all the nations. From Abraham came the nation of Israel; from Israel, the kingly tribe of Judah (Genesis 49); from Judah, the family of Jesse and then David; and from David, the Lord Jesus Christ. And from that, the gospel goes to the whole world. All of that is found in that one promise of Genesis 3:15.

### The Covenant with Noah

The Noahic covenant, sometimes called the Covenant of Preservation, shows God preserving the seed He promised. Even though there was widespread wickedness, the seed needed to continue. God destroys the whole world but saves eight people to be the beginning of His new creation.

### The Covenant with Abraham

After the Tower of Babel — where people attempted to reach heaven by their own efforts, the same temptation Satan had brought to Adam and Eve — God splits humanity by language and then calls Abraham to be the one through whom the seed will come. In Genesis 12, 15, 17, and 22, God gives a progressive revelation of the Abrahamic covenant, each time adding more detail. More and more we get to know God as He gives us more of the promise.

### The Mosaic and Davidic Covenants

In time the family of Abraham grew into the nation of Israel, and through the Mosaic covenant God disclosed more of Himself: His holiness, the requirements for worship, who His people are, how they are identified, what ceremonies He requires. Then through the Davidic covenant, God formally establishes how He will rule among His people, revealing Himself as King — seen through David, a man after God’s own heart, yet deeply flawed — pointing to One who is like David in some ways but not like him in others.

### The New Covenant

The New Covenant is a combination of all prior covenants and goes beyond them. The ultimate revelation God gives us is the Seed of the woman, of Shem, of Abraham, of Judah, of David — Christ Himself, God in the flesh, the greatest revelation of all.

## The Ultimate Revelation: Jesus Christ

The ultimate revelation of God is the incarnation — the Lord Jesus Christ Himself.

We see Him in the Old Testament: Christ is the Creator (John 1:1-5 tells us He was the agent of creation); He is present as the Shekinah glory in the Old Testament, for John 1:14 says *the Word became flesh and dwelt* (*skēnōō* — *shaken* — *among us, and we beheld His glory*). He is veiled in the Old Testament in the ladder that Jacob saw, which John 1:47-51 makes clear is Christ. And throughout the Gospels, Christ revealed the Father through His person, His teaching, and His works.

Turn to Revelation chapter 1, verses 1 through 3:

> *"The revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave Him to show His servants things which must shortly take place... Blessed is he who reads and those who hear the words of this prophecy, and keep those things which are written in it, for the time is near."* (Revelation 1:1-3)

And verses 9-18, where Christ speaks:

> *"I am the Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last... Do not be afraid; I am the First and the Last. I am He who lives, and was dead, and behold, I am alive forevermore. Amen. And I have the keys of Hades and of Death."* (Revelation 1:17-18)

Christ keeps pointing to Himself as the first and the last, the beginning and the end, the final revelation of God. No one can know God apart from knowing Jesus Christ, who is the ultimate and final revelation of God.

As Hebrews 1:1-2 says:

> *"Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son, whom he appointed the heir of all things, through whom also he created the world."* (Hebrews 1:1-2)

## The Modes of Divine Revelation

How did God reveal Himself? Hebrews 1:1 tells us He spoke *in many ways* — so what were those modes?

God spoke through **direct speech** — to Adam, to Noah, to Abraham, to Moses, to Samuel. He spoke through **theophanies** — appearances of God in the Old Testament, especially the Angel of the Lord, who is the second person of the Trinity. When the Angel of the Lord appears to Samson’s parents, they offer a sacrifice and He accepts their worship — which no mere angel ever does (as Revelation makes clear: angels refuse worship). So the Angel of the Lord, who always accepts worship, is the pre-incarnate Christ. He appeared to Abraham, Jacob, Moses, Joshua, Gideon, and many others.

God also spoke through **dreams and visions**: Joseph, Pharaoh, Nebuchadnezzar, Jacob, Daniel, Peter, Isaiah, Paul, and John all received revelatory dreams or visions. He spoke through **angels** (other than the Angel of the Lord): to Abraham in Genesis 18, to Daniel in Daniel 9, to Zechariah, to Mary, to the women at the tomb, to Peter, to Cornelius. He spoke through the **Urim and Thummim** — a device carried by the priest for seeking divine guidance on yes-or-no questions.

He also spoke through **acts of providence**: the manna in the wilderness (which Paul in 1 Corinthians refers to as like the bread of the sacrament), the plagues, the Exodus, the wind that dried up the Red Sea, the vine and the worm in Jonah — all ordinary-looking means through which God revealed something about Himself and about man.

## Scripture as Revelation

All of these modes of revelation — the covenants, the theophanies, the dreams, the prophets, the incarnation itself — resulted in the written Bible. So we consider the Bible as revelation.

You may notice: we are using the Bible to prove the Bible, which is usually considered a circular argument and a logical fallacy. Why do we believe the Bible is the Word of God? Because the Bible says so. Our confession acknowledges this. You can show the beauty of Scripture, how its parts fit together, how there are no mistakes, how prophecies have been fulfilled — but ultimately we believe the Bible is the Word of God because it is a matter of faith, a conviction by the Holy Spirit.

What about the scriptures themselves — are they the Word of God? There are people who say the Bible *contains* the Word of God (among other things). There are those who say it *becomes* the Word of God when the Spirit works through it to a particular reader. But that is not what the Bible teaches about itself. The meaning of Scripture is what it is, whether you exist or not. Everyone has one meaning, and that meaning is true regardless of our response to it.

### The Old Testament’s Witness to Itself

The expression *Thus says the Lord* or *the Word of the Lord came* appears over 2,000 times in the Old Testament. *The word of the Lord* in Isaiah appears over 20 times. *The word of the Lord came to me* runs throughout Jeremiah and Ezekiel. The Old Testament considered itself to be the very words of God.

We also see this high regard in the commands to preserve the books. God commanded Moses to place the stone tablets in the ark of the covenant and the rest of the books beside the ark. Would God have allowed books that are not His word in the ark of the covenant, in the Holy of Holies? No — the very manner of their preservation shows what they thought of themselves.

Look also at the authority of the Torah throughout the first five books of the Bible: this is the Word of God, this is the authority you must follow. In Deuteronomy 17, the king was required to write out the entire book of the law by his own hand and live by it. It presents itself as authoritative.

The prophets spoke authoritatively. 2 Kings 17:13 says:

> *"Yet the Lord warned Israel and Judah by every prophet and every seer, saying, 'Turn from your evil ways and keep my commandments and my statutes, in accordance with all the law that I commanded your fathers, and that I sent to you by my servants the prophets.'"* (2 Kings 17:13)

When the people refused to follow the prophets, they were punished and cursed. The Old Testament saw itself as the authoritative Word of God.

In our next session, we’ll look at what the New Testament says about the Old Testament — and the doctrine of inspiration. Let’s pray. Heavenly Father, thank you that you are good to us, that you give us your Word. We pray that we would rejoice in it. We thank you that it is authoritative. Give us the grace to obey it. We thank you that you have given it to us. We pray that you will open our eyes to see more of you. In Jesus’ name, amen.
